traps.staleText: Trump began [[Second presidency of Donald Trump|his second presidency]] by initiating [[2025 United States federal mass layoffs|mass layoffs of federal workers]]. He [[Tariffs in the second Trump administration|imposed tariffs]] on nearly all countries at the highest level since the [[Great Depression]] and signed the [[One Big Beautiful Bill Act]]. His administration's actions—including the [[Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration|targeting of political opponents and civil society]], [[Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration|persecution of transgender people]], mass [[Deportation in the second Trump administration|deportation of undocumented immigrants]], and extensive use of executive orders—have drawn over 550 [[Legal affairs of the second Trump presidency|lawsuits challenging their legality]]. In Latin America, he pursued a campaign to [[United States strikes on alleged drug traffickers during Operation Southern Spear|attack alleged drug traffickers]], and ordered [[2026 United States intervention in Venezuela|a military raid into Venezuela]] to capture the country's president. In the Middle East, he authorized joint U.S.–Israeli strikes on Iran that began the [[2026 Iran war]].

traps.staleText: Since 2015, Trump's leadership style and political agenda—often referred to as [[Trumpism]]—have reshaped the Republican Party's identity. Many of his comments and actions have been [[Racial views of Donald Trump|characterized as racist]] or misogynistic, which he has denied. He has [[False or misleading statements by Donald Trump|made many false or misleading statements]] during his campaigns and presidency, to a degree unprecedented in American politics, and he [[List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump|promotes conspiracy theories]]. Trump's actions have been described by researchers as authoritarian and contributing to [[Democratic backsliding in the United States|democratic backsliding]]. After his first term, scholars and historians [[#Scholarly rankings|ranked him]] as one of the worst presidents in American history.

traps.staleText: During his first presidency, Trump imposed [[Executive Order 13769|a travel ban]] on seven Muslim-majority countries, expanded the [[Mexico–United States border wall]], and enforced [[Trump administration family separation policy|a family separation policy]] on the border. He rolled back environmental and business regulations, signed the [[Tax Cuts and Jobs Act]], and appointed three Supreme Court justices. In foreign policy, Trump withdrew the U.S. from agreements on climate, trade, and Iran's nuclear program, initiated [[China–United States trade war|a trade war with China]], and engaged in a [[2018–19 Korean peace process|failed peace process in Korea]]. In response to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]] from 2020, he downplayed its severity, contradicted health officials, and signed the [[CARES Act]]. After losing the [[2020 United States presidential election|2020 presidential election]] to [[Joe Biden]], Trump [[Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election|attempted to overturn the result]], culminating in the [[January 6 United States Capitol attack|January 6 Capitol attack]] in 2021. He was [[first impeachment of Donald Trump|impeached in 2019]] for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and [[second impeachment of Donald Trump|in 2021]] for incitement of insurrection; the Senate acquitted him both times.

In foreign policy, Trump withdrew the U.S. from agreements on climate, trade, and Iran's nuclear program, and initiated [[China–United States trade war|a trade war with China]]. In response to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]] from 2020, he downplayed its severity, contradicted health officials, and signed the [[CARES Act]]. After losing the [[2020 United States presidential election|2020 presidential election]] to [[Joe Biden]], Trump [[Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election|attempted to overturn the result]], culminating in the [[January 6 United States Capitol attack|January 6 Capitol attack]] in 2021. He was [[first impeachment of Donald Trump|impeached in 2019]] for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and [[second impeachment of Donald Trump|in 2021]] for incitement of insurrection; the Senate acquitted him both times.

traps.staleText: Since 2015, Trump's leadership style and political agenda—often referred to as [[Trumpism]]—have reshaped the Republican Party's identity. Many of his comments and actions have been [[Racial views of Donald Trump|characterized as racist]] or misogynistic. He has [[False or misleading statements by Donald Trump|made many false or misleading statements]] during his campaigns and presidency, to a degree unprecedented in American politics. He has [[List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump|promoted numerous conspiracy theories]]. Trump's actions, especially in his second term, have been described as authoritarian and contributing to [[Democratic backsliding in the United States|democratic backsliding]]. After his first term, scholars and historians [[Historical rankings of presidents of the United States#Scholar survey summary|ranked him]] as one of the worst presidents in American history.

traps.staleText: During his first presidency, Trump imposed [[Executive Order 13769|a travel ban]] on seven [[Islam_by_country#Countries|Muslim-majority countries]], expanded the [[Mexico–United States border wall]], and enforced [[Trump administration family separation policy|a family separation policy]] on the border. He rolled back environmental and business regulations, signed the [[Tax Cuts and Jobs Act]], and appointed three Supreme Court justices. In foreign policy, Trump withdrew the U.S. from agreements on climate, trade, and Iran's nuclear program, and initiated [[China–United States trade war|a trade war with China]]. In response to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]] from 2020, he downplayed its severity, contradicted health officials, and signed the [[CARES Act]]. After losing the [[2020 United States presidential election|2020 presidential election]] to [[Joe Biden]], Trump [[Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election|attempted to overturn the result]], culminating in the [[January 6 United States Capitol attack|January 6 Capitol attack]] in 2021. He was impeached in 2019 for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and in 2021 for incitement of insurrection; the Senate acquitted him both times.

traps.governingText: '''Donald John Trump''' (born June 14, 1946) is an American politician, media personality, and businessman who is the 47th [[president of the United States]]. A member of the [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican Party]], he served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021.

traps.staleText: The first Trump presidency reduced formal press briefings from about one hundred in 2017 to about half that in 2018 and to two in 2019; they also revoked the press passes of two White House reporters, which were restored by the courts.<ref>{{cite news |last=Grynbaum |first=Michael M. |date=December 30, 2019 |title=After Another Year of Trump Attacks, 'Ominous Signs' for the American Press |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/12/30/business/media/trump-media-2019.html |access-date=October 11, 2021 |work=[[The New York Times]]}}</ref> Trump's 2020 presidential campaign sued ''The New York Times'', ''The Washington Post'', and CNN for defamation in opinion pieces about his stance on Russian election interference. All the suits were dismissed.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2023/may/03/donald-trump-lawsuit-new-york-times-mary-trump |title=US judge throws out Donald Trump's lawsuit against New York Times |date=May 3, 2023 |access-date=November 25, 2024 |work=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref><ref name="Atlantic_Press">{{cite magazine |last1=Geltzer |first1=Joshua A. |last2=Katyal |first2=Neal K. |date=March 11, 2020 |title=The True Danger of the Trump Campaign's Defamation Lawsuits |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2020/03/true-danger-trump-campaigns-libel-lawsuits/607753/ |access-date=October 1, 2020 |magazine=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> By 2024, Trump repeatedly voiced support for [[Criminalization of dissent|outlawing political dissent]] and criticism,<ref>{{cite news |last=Kapur |first=Sahil |date=October 13, 2024 |title='Totally illegal': Trump escalates rhetoric on outlawing political dissent and criticism |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/2024-election/totally-illegal-trump-escalates-rhetoric-outlawing-political-dissent-c-rcna174280 |access-date=November 23, 2024 |work=[[NBC News]]}}</ref> and said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.<ref>{{cite news |last=Folkenflik |first=David |author-link=David Folkenflik |date=October 21, 2024 |title=Could Trump's threats against news outlets carry weight if he wins the presidency? |url=https://www.npr.org/2024/10/21/nx-s1-5150039/could-trumps-threats-against-news-outlets-carry-weight-if-he-wins-the-presidency |access-date=November 23, 2024 |work=[[NPR News]]}}</ref> In July 2025, Paramount parent company to ''60 Minutes'' agreed to pay Trump $16 million USD for its [[Kamala Harris]] story which apparently published inaccurate information about Trump.<ref>CBS: MoneyWatch, "Paramount, President Trump reach $16 million settlement over "60 Minutes" lawsuit". July 2, 2025. [https://www.cbsnews.com/news/paramount-trump-60-minutes-lawsuit-settlement/ ]</ref>

traps.staleText: '''Donald John Trump''' (born June 14, 1946) is an American politician, media personality, and businessman who is currently the 47th [[president of the United States]]. A member of the [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican Party]], he served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021.
traps.governingText: '''Donald John Trump''' (born June 14, 1946) is an American politician, media personality, and businessman who is the 47th [[president of the United States]]. A member of the [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican Party]], he served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021.

tensions.topic: 2016 election result phrasing in the lead
tensions.positionA: Trump won the 2016 election by gaining a majority of the Electoral College, while Hillary Clinton received a larger share of the nationwide popular vote.
tensions.positionB: Trump won the 2016 election by winning a majority of the Electoral College, while Hillary Clinton received over 2.5 million more votes nationwide.
tensions.rfc: Talk:Donald Trump/Archive 37 (Dec 2016); box #15 supersedes #4 — no consensus to change the formulation.

tensions.topic: COVID-19 response wording in the lead
tensions.positionA: Trump reacted slowly to the COVID-19 pandemic; he minimized the threat, ignored or contradicted many recommendations from health officials, and promoted false information about unproven treatments.
tensions.positionB: Trump downplayed the threat of the COVID-19 pandemic; under his administration the United States recorded more confirmed cases than any other country, and the crisis prompted the largest economic stimulus in U.S. history.
tensions.rfc: Talk:Donald Trump/Archive 117 'RfC: Coronavirus in the lead' (Apr–May 2020); box #48 — no consensus on specific wording.

tensions.positionA: Trump never confronted Putin over Russia's alleged bounties for killing American soldiers in Afghanistan.
tensions.positionB: Trump did not confront Putin over Russia's alleged bounties on American soldiers in Afghanistan — allegations later reported by the Biden administration to be held with only low-to-moderate confidence.
tensions.rfc: Talk:Donald Trump/Archive 141 'RfC Russian Bounties claims' (Nov 2021); box #56 — no consensus on alternate wordings.

tensions.positionA: The Abraham Accords were a significant diplomatic achievement of the Trump administration, under which Israel normalized relations with the UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, and Sudan.
tensions.positionB: The Abraham Accords were largely ceremonial agreements between states already at de facto peace, with limited personal involvement from Trump, and are better covered in dedicated foreign-policy articles than emphasized here.
tensions.rfc: Talk:Donald Trump/Archive 166 'RfC for inclusion of Abraham Accords' (2023–24); box #65 — included, no consensus on specific wordings.

tensions.positionA: Joe Biden's biography does not include a dedicated subsection on his gaffes; the proposed section is undue weight for a standalone treatment.
tensions.positionB: Joe Biden's biography should include a subsection on his gaffes; his speech mistakes are extensively covered across the political spectrum and he has called himself a 'gaffe machine'.
tensions.rfc: Talk:Joe Biden/Archive 15 'RfC: Section on gaffes' (Mar 2021, closed No consensus); box #2 — no consensus on including a gaffes subsection.

tensions.positionA: The lead infobox should feature the confirmed-cases-per-capita map most prominently, with the deaths-per-capita map secondary.
tensions.positionB: The lead infobox should feature the deaths-per-capita map most prominently, since deaths are the most significant measure of the pandemic's impact.
tensions.rfc: Talk:COVID-19 pandemic/Archive 33 'Should we switch the lead infobox map…' RfC; box #7 — no consensus on map prominence.
